segments.s183-g49: This is an imperative prohibition. گذر functions as an instruction to 'pass' (here rendered as 'pass through'), followed by the prepositional phrase زی کلات ('through/at Kalat') where زی is the archaic/prepositional variant of از/به meaning 'from/through'. ایچ گونه ('in no such manner') is a negative-manner phrase (ایچ = 'not at all', گونه = 'manner'), and مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. Word order is verb (imperative) + location + manner + negative particle, typical of poetic diction.
segments.s183-g50: This is a conditional clause introduced by گر ('if'). آن ره ('that road/that course') is the conditional topic; روی here functions as a relational element with the road (in classical usage it can mark travelling/going on a path or 'upon'), خام is a predicate adjective 'raw/immature/rash', and گردد is the present-subjunctive/optative form of گشتن used as 'becomes'. سخن ('speech/words') is the subject or theme that takes the predicate 'becomes rash'.
segments.s183-g51: روان سیاوش ('the soul of Siyavash') is a nominative phrase; چو خورشید ('like the sun') makes a simile introduced by چو ('as/like'). باد at the end is the optative particle 'may' wishing the preceding state to obtain (classical poetic use). The order is nominal phrase + simile + optative.
segments.s183-g52: بدان گیتیش جای امید باد is an optative clause continuing the wish. ب is the prepositional prefix (equivalent to به 'to/in'), followed by دان (here problematic in the glossing — likely part of the fused expression بدَان = 'in/there' or an older demonstrative); گیتیش = 'his/world' (گیت = world with a possessive/formal ending), جای = 'place', امید = 'hope', and باد again is the optative 'may'. The whole means roughly 'may there be for him in that world a place of hope.'
segments.s183-g53: This clause states possession/existence with the archaic impersonal construction: پسر بودش = 'a son was to him' (= 'he had a son'). از دخت پیران ('from/of the daughter of Piran') gives the mother's identity; یکی 'one' specifies number. Note that پیران here is a proper name (Piran) in the Shahnameh context rather than the common plural 'elders'.
segments.s183-g54: This relative clause begins with که ('who/which/that') and predicates about the son: پیدا نبود 'was not known/visible' (پیدا = 'found/known', نبود = past negative of 'to be'), از پدر 'from the father' indicates provenance, and اندکی 'a little/at all' here emphasizes scarcity or lack. The whole means the son was not clearly recognized as from the father (i.e., his paternity was not obvious/acknowledged).
segments.s183-g55: برادر به من نیز ماننده بود literally 'a brother was like me as well.' برادر is the noun 'brother', به من 'to me' functions idiomatically as 'of mine' or 'compared to me', نیز 'also', ماننده 'similar' (an adjective/participial form meaning 'like'), and بود is the past copula 'was'. The phrase expresses resemblance/relationship.
segments.s183-g56: A simple descriptive sequence: جوان بود 'he was young' (جوان + past copula), و همسال 'and of the same age' (همسال functioning as an adjective/noun 'peer'), و فرخنده بود 'and (his name/was) Farrokhandeh' or 'and was auspicious/fortunate' (فرخنده is either an epithet/name); the repeated بود marks separate qualities in poetic style.
segments.s183-g9: A causal/agentive prepositional phrase headed by the preposition از (here 'from/because of'). آواز اسپان and گرد سپاه are noun-noun constructions: 'آواز اسپان' = 'the sound/call of the horses' and 'گرد سپاه' = 'the dust of the army' (Persian often juxtaposes the possessor and possessed without an overt ezafe in poetry).
segments.s183-g10: بشد is the past/preterite (or resultative) of شدن 'to become' used impersonally/third-person ('it became' / 'was made'). The predicate is the adjective phrase قیرگون روی خورشید و ماه = 'the face(s) of sun and moon pitch‑black' where قیرگون modifies روی; classical word order places the verb before the following nominal predicate for emphasis.
segments.s183-g11: Another causal phrase introduced by ز (poetic variant of از) listing two sources: چاک سلیح = 'the splitting/rending of weapons' (چاک as a noun 'a split/cleaving' applied to سلیح 'arms') and ز آوای پیل = 'from the sound/cry of the elephant' (آوا as 'sound/voice' governing پیل).
segments.s183-g12: تو گفتی is the simple past 'you said'. بیاگند is an optative/imperative-like formation (بیا- + گَند) expressing 'let [something] become/grow rotten or be made so' used here figuratively; گیتی به نیل is the object/result phrase 'the world (گیتی) into/unto indigo (نیل)' where به marks the result/state the speaker invokes. Overall: 'you cried/saying, "let the world be (made) into indigo/blackened."'
segments.s183-g13: A nominal subject (هوا 'the air/sky') followed by a series of predicative color adjectives joined by و (no copula is needed in Persian poetry): 'سرخ و زرد و کبود و بنفش' = 'red and yellow and azure and purple' describing the atmosphere/sky.
segments.s183-g14: A causal/ablative phrase introduced by ز 'from/because of' plus an infinitival nounial use of تابیدن 'to shine' — تابیدن کاویانی درفش = 'the shining of the Kayanian banner'. کاویانی qualifies درفش (Kayanian banner), so the phrase reads 'from the shining of the Kayanian banner'.
segments.s183-g15: بگردش here functions as a locative/temporal noun-phrase equivalent to به گردش 'at the turning/circling' (classical poetic form). It governs the following genitive phrase سواران گودرزیان 'the riders/horsemen of the Gudarzis' — together: 'at the circling of the Gudarzi horsemen.'
segments.s183-g16: میان اندرون is a doubled locative ('in the midst/within the inner part') followed by the noun phrase اختر کاویان 'the Kayanian star' (اختر modified by کاویان). The whole unit places that star 'in the inner midst' — 'میان' = 'among/midst', 'اندرون' = 'inside/inner quarters'.
segments.s183-g33: بپایست is the classical/jussive form (ب‑ + ایست) meaning 'stand/stand firm' in an exhortative sense. با is the preposition 'with', and اختر کاویان is a noun phrase 'the Kayanian star' (اختر = 'star', کاویان = Kayanian/patrician dynastic epithet). The whole segment is an imperative/exhortation: 'Stand with the Kayanian star.'
segments.s183-g34: بفرمان او is a prepositional phrase 'by his order' (بـ + فرمان = 'by order of'); بست is the verb 'to bind/fasten' (here non-finite/past-stem used with modality), باید expresses necessity ('must' / 'it is necessary'); میان ('between') appears at the end by poetic word order. Together they mean 'by his order a bond must be fastened between (them)'.
segments.s183-g35: بدو = به + او 'to him' (indirect object). داد is the past tense 'gave'. مهری here is a gift/token of favour (a token/amulet), and به پیش سپاه = 'before/in the presence of the army' (به پیش = 'in front of' or 'before', سپاه = 'army, host').
segments.s183-g36: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'for/that'. سالار = 'commander, chief'. اویست here is an archaic/contracted form (written as اویست) corresponding to 'اوست' = 'he is' (the given sense list treats اویست as a name/epithet, but syntactically it functions as 'is he' here). و links the second predicate; جوینده راه = 'seeker of the road' i.e. 'guide'. The clause explains why the gift was given.
segments.s183-g37: بفرمان او = 'by his command.' بود باید combines the past auxiliary بود with باید to express a past necessity/obligation ('it was necessary' / 'he was to'), and همه = 'all, everyone'. The clause sets up an obligation that follows in the next segment.
segments.s183-g38: کجا here functions as 'where' / 'wherever.' بندها = 'the bonds/fetters'. ز is the poetic form of 'از' ('from'/'of') and with the verb گشاید (present/subjunctive 'opens/loosens') yields 'he loosens (from)'; همه at the end reiterates 'all.' In combination with the previous segment, the meaning is 'wherever the bonds are, he shall loosen them all.'
segments.s183-g39: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said'). مگذر ز is a negative imperative/prohibitive: م‑ prefix negates گذر (to pass/ to transgress), so مگذر ز پیمان من = 'do not transgress my covenant' ('ز/از' marks 'from' or 'of' in the phrase 'from my covenant' = 'break my covenant').
segments.s183-g40: نگه‌دار can be read as an imperative 'keep/preserve' (though listed senses include 'guardian/preserver'); آیین و فرمان من = 'my ritual/law and my command' (آیین = custom/ritual, فرمان = command). Together it is an exhortation: 'Preserve my practice and my decree.'
segments.s183-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. بنمود is the past of نمودن (classical), here intransitive 'showed/appeared' (literally 'made visible'). بالای خویش is a possessive noun phrase 'its height/brow/top' (بالای = 'height/upper place', خویش is the possessive formed from خوی + ـش), so the whole segment means 'when the sun showed its height'.
segments.s183-g2: نشست is the simple past (3rd sg.) of نشستن 'sat' (here 'sat down' / 'dismounted'). از بر is a compound prepositional phrase meaning 'from off' or 'down from upon' (از = 'from', بر = 'on/upon'). تند is an adjective 'swift/fierce' modifying the following noun phrase; بالای خویش again means 'his high seat/place' with the enclitic ـش marking possession. Together: 'he sat down from off his swift high seat.'
segments.s183-g3: بزیر is an adverbial phrase 'downwards / downward'. اندر آورد is a compound verb (اندر + آورد) meaning 'brought in / brought down' (آورد, here 'bring/cause'); برج بره is the object 'the tower/sign of the ram' (classical phrase for the zodiac sign Aries). Word order places the directional adverb before the verb: 'he brought (it) downward, the ram's sign.'
segments.s183-g4: چنین is a connective adverb 'thus/so'. تا here introduces the result clause meaning 'so that/thus'. زمین is the subject 'the earth'. زرد is the predicate adjective 'yellow', and شد is the past of شدن 'became'. یکسره is an adverb 'completely/entirely' modifying the change. Together: 'thus the earth became entirely yellow.'
segments.s183-g5: برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. درگاه طوس is a genitive (the gate/threshold of Tus). تبیره is the noun introducing what rose; its exact classical senses are uncertain in the provided list (here it functions semantically as 'a clamour/noise/uproar'), so its sense index is marked -1 because the given sense options do not match this usage.
segments.s183-g6: همان is 'that/the same' pointing back to the sound just mentioned. ناله is 'a wail/cry/lament'. بوق is 'trumpet/horn'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. آوای is the construct form 'the sound/voice of...' and کوس is 'war-drum'. The sequence lists the components of the clamour: 'that wail of trumpets and the sound of drums.'
segments.s183-g7: ز is the poetic 'from' (here introducing source). کشور is 'the country/land/realm'. برآمد is 'arose/issued forth'. سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'throughout/entire'. خروش is 'a cry/roar/uproar'. The clause literally reads 'From the land arose an all-over uproar' (i.e. 'a tumult arose throughout the land').
segments.s183-g8: زمین is 'the earth'. پرخروش is a compound adjective (پر- + خروش) meaning 'full of roar/turbulent'. و is 'and'. هوا is 'the air/atmosphere'. پر + ز + جوش is a periphrastic expression 'full (پر) of (ز) joosh' — جوش here 'surge/boiling/ardor', so 'the air full of surging'. Word order places the adjectival/full-of expressions after the nouns they modify.
segments.s183-g25: The past verb برفتند (they went) starts the line (verb-first word order common in poetry) and is modified by the adverb یکسر (all/at once). چو introduces a simile 'like' and precedes the noun phrase کوهی سیاه ('a black mountain'), where کوهی functions as a nominal/simile element and سیاه is its adjective. Together this segment means 'they went all at once like a black mountain.'
segments.s183-g26: This is a coordinated noun phrase (گرازان و تازان) followed by a locative phrase بنزدیک شاه. The earlier verb برفتند governs these nouns: 'boars and raiders (came) near the king.' بنزدیک is a poetic/compound locative meaning 'close to' and stands immediately before its object شاه.
segments.s183-g27: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he said/commanded' and heads the clause. تا introduces the subordinate/purpose clause 'that/so that', and نامداران 'the renowned ones' is the noun phrase (object/target of the order). گرد here functions as a circumstantial/collective marker ('around' / 'together'), indicating gathering of those named.
segments.s183-g28: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; لشکر is 'the army/host' and سپهبد identifies the commander (spahbed) associated with that army. سوی introduces direction 'toward / to', شاه is the destination 'the king'. برد here would normally be the past of بردن 'to take/bring', but the provided sense entries do not include that verbal sense (see senses); syntactically the phrase says the men were taken/brought to the king.
segments.s183-g29: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when', followed by the subject لشکر همه 'the whole army'. نزد شاه is a locative/presence phrase 'in the presence of the king / before the king', and آمدند is the simple past plural verb 'they came'. Word order is typical: temporal marker + subject + locative + verb.
segments.s183-g30: This clause continues with the accompanying detail: دمان here heads a collective/positional phrase (literally 'skirt/edge' in the lexicon but used poetically to mean a body/array approaching). با introduces the instruments/accoutrements 'with', followed by درفش 'banner' and کلاه 'helmet' joined by و, and the plural verb آمدند repeats to show they 'arrived/advanced' with those items.
segments.s183-g31: بدیشان 'to them' is an indirect-object/adverbial prepositional-like form. چنین 'thus' prepares the quoted or reported speech. گفت would normally be the verb 'said', but the provided sense list gives only discourse-noun senses, so its verbal sense is marked separately. بیدار 'awake/alert' is an epithet placed before شاه 'the king' (adjectival modifier after the verb in this poetic ordering), giving 'the wakeful king said thus to them.'
segments.s183-g32: که here is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content of what the king said. طوس names the hero Tūs (proper name) and سپهبد gives his title 'spahbed'. به پیش سپاه is a prepositional/directional phrase 'to/at the front of the army' (به + پیش + سپاه), indicating the position Tūs is to take. The clause is a straightforward reported-order: 'that Tūs, the spahbed, (go) to the front of the army.'
segments.s183-g57: کنون is an adverb ‘now’ placed at the front. در + کلاتست = در کلات + است where the copula است is suffixed to کلات as کلاتست (poetic contraction) giving ‘is in Kalat’. و با مادرست repeats the same pattern: با (with) + مادر + است contracted to مادرست, i.e. ‘is with (his) mother’ — the verb/copula is incorporated into the noun forms.
segments.s183-g58: جهانجوی is a nominal epithet ‘world-seeking’ (a person who seeks the world). It is followed by two prepositional phrases با فر and با لشکرست; the second contains the copula fused as لشکرست (لشکر + است) meaning ‘(he is) with an army’. The two با-phrases enumerate qualities/companions: with glory (فر) and with a host (لشکر).
segments.s183-g59: نداند is a negative 3rd-person present ‘does not know’ (classical spelling). The phrase is inverted: کسی را نداند = ‘no one knows him’ (کسی here under negation is best understood as ‘no one’). ز ایران is the source/region ‘from Iran’, and بنام here means ‘by name / called’, so the whole clause means ‘no one from Iran knows him by name.’
segments.s183-g60: ازان سو is an adverbial phrase ‘from that side/direction’ (از + آن + سو, here contracted). نباید is an impersonal modal ‘one must not/it should not’, followed by the infinitive کشیدن ‘to draw/pull’ and its object لگام ‘bridle’; Persian uses نباید + infinitive to express prohibition: ‘from that side one must not draw the bridle.’
segments.s183-g61: سپه دارد is straightforward: سپه ‘army/host’ + دارد ‘has/possesses’ (the subject is implied from context). The conjunction و links a second noun phrase نامداران جنگ ‘renowned men/warriors of war’ that is also governed by دارد (elliptical repetition): ‘he has a host and renowned warriors.’
segments.s183-g62: یکی functions as an indefinite numeral ‘a certain/one’ (here introducing an item), followed by کوه ‘mountain’. بر is the preposition ‘on/upon’ and راه دشوار و تنگ is the noun راه ‘road’ modified postpositively by the adjectives دشوار ‘difficult’ and تنگ ‘narrow’ (adjectives follow the noun in poetic/classical Persian). The whole phrase means ‘a certain mountain on the difficult, narrow road.’
segments.s183-g63: همو ‘he himself / that very one’ is emphatic. مرد جنگست = مرد + جنگ + است (copula contracted as جنگست) = ‘he is a man of war/warrior.’ The following و introduces گرد و سوار ‘troops and horsemen’; the verb is elliptical (the copula or possession from the previous clause extends to these nouns), so the phrase says he is a warrior and (has/commands) troops and horsemen.
segments.s183-g64: بگوهر is a poetic contraction of به گوهر / ‘adorned with gems’ (ب = به). بزرگ follows as the adjective ‘great/large’. و separates the next phrase بتن نامدار, where بتن = به تن (‘on the body’, another contracted classical form) + نامدار ‘renowned’ — together describing the troops/horsemen as ‘richly jeweled and famed in their bearing/appearance.’
segments.s183-g65: براه بیابان is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning 'on/along the wilderness road' (براه = 'on the way, en route' + بیابان = 'desert/wilderness'). بباید is an archaic/modal present meaning 'it is necessary' or 'one must'; it governs the following verbal element. شدن here functions as an infinitival/passive-type verbal element ('to be done / to proceed') so the whole chunk reads 'one must proceed on the wilderness road.' Word order is typical older/poetic Persian: locative phrase first, then modal + verbal infinitive.
segments.s183-g66: نه نیکو بود is a negative past judgment: نه + نیکو + بود = 'it was not good.' راه شیران زدن is a noun phrase 'راه شیران' ('the lions' path' i.e. the way of brave men) plus the verb زدن. In older/poetic Persian زدن can mean 'to take/strike out on' a road (cf. 'راه زدن' = 'to set out'); here it means 'to take/follow the path.' Overall: 'it was not good to take the lions' way.'
segments.s183-g67: چنین گفت پس is a short sequence 'thus said then' (چنین = 'thus/so'; پس = 'then/afterwards'). طوس با شهریار = 'Tūs with the king' (i.e. 'Tūs said to the king'). گفت is the past tense 'said' (the provided lexical senses did not include the simple past meaning, so the sense index is not given below). The clause places the manner first (چنین), then the verb and participants in typical poetic order.
segments.s183-g68: که introduces a reported content clause: 'that ...'. از رای تو is a genitive phrase 'from your counsel' or 'according to your counsel' (از = 'from/of', رای = 'counsel/plan', تو = 'you'). نگذرد is present/subjunctive/optative here meaning 'will not pass / will not go beyond' (ن- prefix for negation + گذرد 'to pass'); روزگار is used as 'fate'/'fortune' so the clause says 'that fate will not go against your counsel.' Word order: subordinate particle + prepositional phrase + verb + subject-noun (poetic flexibility).
segments.s183-g69: براهی روم is a locative/directional phrase 'on the way to Rum' (براهی = 'on the road/way', روم = 'Rum/Byzantium'). کم تو فرمان دهی is a clause with کم ('few, little') modifying فرمان دهی ('you give commands'); دهی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive/imperative form 'you give'—in older Persian such clauses can express a conditional admonition ('if/when I go to Rum, give few commands'). Word order places the adverbial/directional first, then the comment/advice.
segments.s183-g70: نیاید ز فرمان تو جز بهی is an evaluative result clause: نیاید = 'will not come/appear' (negative form of آمدن); ز = 'from'; فرمان تو = 'your command'; جز = 'except'; بهی = 'good/benefit.' Together: 'from your command nothing will come except good.' Note the classical/poetic negation نیاید and the restrictive جز; the clause affirms that only good will spring from the king's orders.
segments.s183-g41: The segment states a prohibition about behavior on the way. 'باید' is the modal 'must/ought'; 'کسی را' is the definite/object-marked 'someone' (را marks the person as object). 'براه' is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'on the road / en route'. The initial form 'نیازرد' is an archaic/poetic verb form placed before the modal for emphatic, negative sense (here understood as 'not to harm'); no provided dictionary sense matches this verbal use.
segments.s183-g42: 'چنینست' is a copular/explanatory phrase 'thus it is' introducing the general rule; the following nominal phrase 'آیین تخت و کلاه' is the subject/predicate 'the custom/practice of throne and hat' (تخت و کلاه together = kingship/crown). Word order is predicate ('چنینست') + nominal explanation.
segments.s183-g43: 'کشاورز‌گر' is a conditional phrase formed with the enclitic 'گر' = 'if' attached to 'کشاورز' ('if (they are) farmers'). The list 'مردم پیشه‌ور' names classes of people — 'the people, (or) tradesmen/artisans'. Together this gives the protasis 'If the people are farmers or artisans'.
segments.s183-g44: This apodosis: 'کسی کو' uses 'کو' as a relative/particle 'who/that'; 'بلشکر' = 'with an army' (ب + لشکر), and 'نبندد کمر' is a negative jussive/subjunctive 'should not gird the waist' (i.e. should not take up arms). The verb 'نبندد' comes before 'کمر' (object) as a common Classical Persian order.
segments.s183-g45: 'نباید' is impersonal prohibition 'must not / should not'; 'که' introduces the subordinate clause; the verb phrase 'وزد باد' shows a Classical inversion where the verb 'وزد' ('blow') precedes its noun 'باد' ('wind') — 'باد سرد' = 'cold wind'; 'بر وی' = 'upon him'. Together: 'A cold wind should not blow upon him.'
segments.s183-g46: 'مکوش' is an imperative/negative injunction 'do not attempt / strive'; 'ایچ' (archaic) intensifies negation 'at all / nothing'; 'جز' = 'except'; 'با کسی همنبرد' is a prepositional phrase 'with someone as fellow-combatant' (همنبرد = companion-in-battle). The whole segment says: 'Do not attempt anything except joining in battle with a comrade.'
segments.s183-g47: 'نباید' again gives an impersonal prohibition 'one should not'; followed by the infinitive 'نمودن' 'to show / to cause'. 'ببی' is a respectful term 'lady' (object of the verb). The reduplication 'رنج رنج' is emphatic 'suffering after suffering / repeated suffering'.
segments.s183-g48: 'که' introduces the resulting or purposive clause; 'بر کس نماند' = 'remain to no one / be left for anyone' with 'نماند' in the negative/optative sense 'may not remain'; 'سرای سپنج' is a nominal phrase 'house/bed of padding (سپنج = sponge/pad)' — here a poetic expression for a cushioned dwelling or bed. The clause warns against causing such hardship that people are left without even a cushioned place.
segments.s183-g17: This is a nominal phrase giving the subject: سپهدار (spahbed/commander) followed by با + a coordinated list of equipment. با here means 'with' and introduces items that accompany or belong to the commander. The items are linked by و (and) in the usual right-to-left order: افسر (armour/crown/helmet), گرز (mace), نای (ney, reed-flute).
segments.s183-g18: بیامد is a past-tense/simple perfect verb 'came' (classical/poetic form instead of آمد). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before the following locative phrase. بالای پرده‌سرای is a prepositional/locative unit meaning 'the top/above of the curtain-house (stage/inner-hall)'; بالای is 'on/above' and پرده‌سرا is a compound denoting the curtained hall or stage area.
segments.s183-g19: بشد here is a classical past/achievement sense 'became/rose/appeared' (it introduces Tus's action). طوس is the proper name (Tūs) and is the subject; با کاویانی درفش is an instrumental/associative prepositional phrase 'with the Kayanian banner/standard' describing what he bore or stood with.
segments.s183-g20: This is a circumstantial participial construction: بپای ... کرده + [زرینه کفش]. Here بپای functions idiomatically as 'به‌پای' (on his feet / having put on) and together with کرده (the perfect participle 'having done/put on') yields 'having put on golden shoes'; اندرون marks the target location (inner chamber). زرینه precedes کفش as an adjective 'golden' modifying 'shoes'.
segments.s183-g21: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' / 'one'. پیل پیکر is a compound adjective 'elephant-bodied' (پیل 'elephant' + پیکر 'bodied/formed') modifying درفش 'banner/standard'. از is the preposition 'from' and برش (here 'part/side/profile') indicates the point of origin ('from its side/part').
segments.s183-g22: بابر is a proper name (here the agent bringing the banner). اندر آورده is the compound verbal phrase 'brought in' (اندر + آوردن = bring into). تابان is an attributive participle 'shining/gleaming' that modifies the following noun; سرش is 'its/his head' (سر + possessive suffix ش), describing that what gleamed was the head.
segments.s183-g23: This is a relative clause introduced by که modifying بزرگان 'the nobles/great ones'. با marks accompaniment or possession ('with') and links the ornaments طوق (neck-collar/torc) and افسر (here an ornamental headpiece/crown/helmet) joined by و. بدند is an archaic plural past form 'they were' / 'they had' indicating the nobles possessed those items.
segments.s183-g24: جهانجوی is an adjective/epithet 'world-seeking' or 'ambitious' describing the same nobles. وز here is an archaic connective/prepositional element linking the epithet to the following genitive phrase; تخم نوذر is a genitive 'seed/lineage of Nowzar' (i.e. descendants of Nowzar). The final بدند repeats the archaic plural predicate 'they were' (they belonged to/they were of that lineage).

sentences.s183-s25: Do not pass through Kalat in this way; if you travel that road, your speech will become rash.
sentences.s183-s26: May Siyavash's soul be like the sun; may there be for him in that world a place of hope.
sentences.s183-s27: He had one son by Piran's daughter, who was not at all known to be by his father.
sentences.s183-s28: He had a brother much like me; he was young, of the same age, and named Farrokhandeh (auspicious).
sentences.s183-s5: From the cries of the horses and the dust of the host, the faces of the sun and the moon became pitch-black.
sentences.s183-s6: From the tearing of arms and from the sound of the elephant's cry, you said, 'Let the world be turned to indigo.'
sentences.s183-s7: The air was red and yellow and dark-blue and purple from the shining of the Kayanian banner.
sentences.s183-s8: At the circling of the Gudarzi horsemen, the Kayanian star was in the inner midst.
sentences.s183-s17: Let the Kayanian star stand with him; by his command a bond must be fastened between them.
sentences.s183-s18: He gave him a token before the army, for he is its commander and a guide on the road.
sentences.s183-s19: By his command it was necessary that wherever the bonds were, he should loosen them all.
sentences.s183-s20: He said to him, 'Do not break my covenant; keep my law and command.'
sentences.s183-s1: When the sun revealed its height, he sat down from off his swift high seat.
sentences.s183-s2: He brought the ram's sign down; thus the earth became wholly yellow.
sentences.s183-s3: From Tus's gate there rose a clamour — the same wailing of trumpets and the sound of drums.
sentences.s183-s4: Throughout the land an uproar arose; the earth was full of tumult and the air full of surging.
sentences.s183-s13: They set off all at once like a black mountain; boars and raiders rushed up close to the king.
sentences.s183-s14: He commanded that the renowned men be gathered from the army — the spahbed — and brought before the king.
sentences.s183-s15: When the whole army had come before the king, they arrived in ranks with banners and helmets.
sentences.s183-s16: The king, awake, said thus to them: 'Tūs the spahbed should advance before the army.'
sentences.s183-s29: Now he is in Kalat with his mother; (he is) a seeker of the world, with glory and with an army.
sentences.s183-s30: No one from Iran knows him by name; from that side one must not draw the bridle.
sentences.s183-s31: He has a host and renowned warriors of war; a certain mountain lies on the difficult, narrow road.
sentences.s183-s32: He himself is a man of war, and (has) troops and horsemen, richly jeweled and renowned in their appearance.
sentences.s183-s33: One must go by the wilderness road; it is not good to take the lions' way.
sentences.s183-s34: Thus Tūs then said to the king that fate would not pass beyond your counsel.
sentences.s183-s35: If I set out for Rum, give but few commands; from your command nothing will come except good.
sentences.s183-s21: One must not hurt anyone on the road; such is the custom of throne and crown.
sentences.s183-s22: If the people are farmers or craftsmen, no one should gird his loins with an army.
sentences.s183-s23: A cold wind should not blow upon him; do not strive at all except to take up arms with a comrade.
sentences.s183-s24: One must not make a lady endure repeated suffering, so that no person's padded dwelling remains (i.e. so people are left without even a cushioned bed).
sentences.s183-s9: The spahbed (commander), with a helmet and a mace and a ney, came from atop the curtain-hall.
sentences.s183-s10: Tus rose with the Kayanian standard, having put on golden shoes for the inner chamber.
sentences.s183-s11: A banner the size of an elephant was brought in from its side — Babur brought it in, its head gleaming.
sentences.s183-s12: The nobles — who wore collars and helmets — were world-seeking; they were of the seed of Nowzar.
